## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BZ
2	
3	## Boundary — shape, not mechanism (cinched)
4	
5	This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current keyfile, no-org operator-credential tier —
6	restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's. The backing
7	stays keyfile; a no-org operator runs it unchanged. We do **not** touch federation here.
8	OUT of scope: Workforce pool/provider setup, STS exchange, IdP device flow, RBRD mode-enum
9	activation — federation's own future work (RBSHR "Operator federation"; memo-20260527).
10	
11	The payoff is structural, not nominal: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke,
12	the declared ledger, the audit) becomes tier-blind, so a later federation switch reshapes nothing
13	in it — it swaps identity-kind (citizen → federate) and token mint (signed-JWT → STS) and nothing
14	else.
15	
16	Mechanism for everything below lives in `Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md`; this
17	paddock holds shape only.
18	
19	NOTE — supersedes memo-20260527: it says operator verbs "keep their names, only bodies differ" and
20	keyfile is "one SA key file each per role." This heat converges the verb *bodies* in shape, makes
21	the citizen tier one SA *per person*, and generalizes 20260527's role-keyed token seam to an
22	identity-keyed one. (A deferral-honoring breadcrumb is on memo-20260527 now; full note when frozen.)
23	
24	## The citizen model — identity decoupled from capability
25	
26	- **Citizen** = a keyfile-tier operator identity (governor/director/retriever), rank-neutral: rank
27	  is not a property of the citizen, it lives in the capability-sets it holds. Multi-role is
28	  first-class — one citizen holds several capability-sets. (Federation peer = *federate*; payor =
29	  founder, outside both. Definitions: memo.)
30	- **Identity lifecycle is decoupled from capability lifecycle** — the spine. Adding/removing a
31	  citizen is independent of granting/revoking its capabilities. Today's `invest` fuses the two;
32	  the model unfuses them. (Why this is achievable: memo.)
33	- **Capability-set** = a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (lifted out of the invest
34	  bodies). Tier-blind.
35	- **The verbs dissolve** to add-citizen / remove-citizen / grant / revoke / rekey + read-ledger,
36	  parameterized by (actor, capability-set, citizen). invest/divest/mantle/roster stop existing as
37	  distinct concepts.
38	- **Capability verbs are tier-blind; only identity verbs branch by tier** (and by the payor's
39	  OAuth backing). rekey is keyfile-only.
40	- **Grant/revoke operate only on named capability-sets, never raw bindings** — the contamination
41	  guard that keeps the ledger auditable.
42	- Ontology: the depot's civic ontology covers **operator** identities — citizens (and later
43	  federates), people who hold capabilities — and **holdings** (arks/hoards/bullions), property.
44	  Cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it. ("citizen" supersedes
45	  RBSHR's prose use of it for an artifact, now reworded to "holding.")
46	
47	## Naming & the home — Manor, Census (settled this session)
48	
49	The federation ledger's "home" is satisfied without inventing a polity abstraction (a framing
50	explored and dropped):
51	
52	- **Manor = the Payor project — concrete, singular, one-of.** The administrative seat: the one GCP
53	  project hosting the Payor SA, OAuth client, and billing, under which Depots are funded. NOT a
54	  cross-cloud polity. Now a first-class RBS0 quoin (`rbtgi_manor`, cult name of the former
55	  `rbtgi_payor_project`), its singularity made explicit so it stops being forgotten.
56	- **Census = the declared ledger** (the intent registry; "Census" = the citizen-roll-with-rank,
57	  bonded to *citizen*). The **Manor hosts the Census** — resolving the ledger-home question: it
58	  lives in the Payor project where ledger-write authority already sits. Recording AWS-targeting
59	  intent in the Census is not administering AWS (asymmetric sovereignty intact; Manor stays
60	  GCP-only). No grand polity concept unless multi-tenancy / multi-Manor becomes real.
61	
62	Reserved civic-ontology quoins — named here, NOT yet in RBS0 (premature pre-MVP): **Census**
63	(ledger), **Citizen** (operator-altitude identity, not a role), **capability-set** (cult name TBD —
64	portfolio/office). A new RBS0 category prefix likely houses the civic ontology when incorporated.
65	
66	Verb dispositions (gestalt-adjust where the word's soul survives; before MVP, no legacy — the gate
67	is semantic fit, not legacy cost):
68	
69	- **invest/divest → adjust in place** to capability grant/revoke (role-keyed `rbtgo_*_invest`
70	  quoins collapse to one role-generic verb).
71	- **charter/knight → retire** (role-bound). **roster → retire** (dissolves into reading the
72	  Census). **mantle → TBD.**
73	- **membership (add/remove citizen) → mint** civic verbs: **enfranchise / expel**.
74	
75	## Intent vs enforcement — the declared ledger (cinched)
76	
77	Enforcement is server-side IAM, always. Intent — which citizen is meant to hold which capability —
78	is stored in a depot-resident **declared ledger**, distinct from IAM. The audit is the diff between
79	**actual IAM state** and the declared ledger; the routine ledger read is identical across tiers.
80	Federation forces the same ledger anyway, so it is paid once for both. (Today's name-regex `roster`
81	verb is retired — no prefix to filter on. Why it must be stored, and where it lives: memo.)
82	
83	## The audit — drift as signal, asymmetric healing (cinched)
84	
85	The audit measures drift between declared intent (ledger) and IAM. Healing is asymmetric, and the
86	asymmetry is the Pale recognition test:
87	
88	- **Deficit** (IAM short of intent — our own grant failed) is ours → auto-converge, logged.
89	- **Surplus** (IAM beyond intent — touched outside our verbs) crossed the Pale → report; a human
90	  adjudicates. The audit never auto-revokes; surplus resolution is human-driven.
91	
92	Both capability directions write intent first — grant writes the ledger before adding bindings,
93	revoke withdraws it before removing them — so a crash lands as a safe report-only surplus, never an
94	auto-converging deficit. Auto-converge is safe only where ledger-write authority is at least as
95	protected as grant (else a softer escalation path; see Open). And the audit reads **both
96	by-identity and by-resource-member**, so a grant to an unrostered principal cannot hide. (Drift
97	taxonomy, the member-first axis, definition-change gating, concurrency, and the orphan marker:
98	memo.)
99	
100	## Authority topology — the one seam with policy content
101	
102	"Who may grant which capability-set." Shape:
103	
104	- Authority boundaries are IAM-real only where they fall on resource-scope splits (payor/governor
105	  holds because billing is a separate resource; governor/director does not).
106	- Grant authority is preventively scopable at **project** scope but only **detectively** at
107	  **resource** scope — so the resolution composes: IAM-limit the project-scoped grants,
108	  detective-audit the resource-scoped residual, and pull authority to the payor where a standing
109	  governor buys nothing.
110	- **Tier-dependent**: keyfile/solo → topology (no/minimal standing governor); federation/multi-admin
111	  → a condition-limited governor. The model allows the answer to differ (actor and capability-set
112	  are parameters).
113	- Surplus-detection's security value depends on the topology — do not substitute detective audit for
114	  structural closure. (The IAM attribute, its scope/role limits, and the seam reasoning: memo.)
115	
116	## Blast radius — larger resting target, finer response (cinched)
117	
118	One citizen = one SA = one key carrying the union of held capabilities — forced by "one identity per
119	person," accepted as a tradeoff. The resting-key form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only
120	(federation has no key at rest; the union property itself is tier-blind); most citizens wear one
121	hat; and decoupling buys finer incident response (revoke a capability without touching the key;
122	rekey without touching capabilities). (Reasoning: memo.)
123	
124	## Inherited concern — governor teardown leak
125	
126	Governor teardown sits inside this heat's blast radius: today's mantle leaks a project tombstone
127	per re-mantle. The convergence closes it (cheap revoke-before-delete; an idempotent governor is a
128	further, not-free refinement). Mechanism + standalone fallback if descoped:
129	memo + memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.
130	
131	## What done looks like
132	
133	Every cult-verb surface migrated to the citizen/capability vocabulary: workstation bash (role-keyed
134	selection collapses to one identity accessor), the governor/payor admin verbs (the admin verbs
135	collapse to the dissolved set), cloud-side bash naming roles, the .adoc specs voicing the cult
136	verbs, the Rust theurge cases, the onboarding handbook. "citizen" replaces the role-named identity
137	throughout, and the SA name itself becomes identity-only (the naming migration: memo).
138	
139	The load-bearing federation-enabling core is narrow: route the role/file-path-keyed token-accessor
140	calls through one identity-keyed accessor (generalizing memo-20260527's "single code seam"). The
141	vocabulary convergence is the larger, motivated-but-not-required remainder. Scoping/heat-chopping is
142	deferred — recorded here as shape.
143	
144	## Cinched decisions
145	
146	- Enforcement is server-side IAM. No file or client-side check is ever the boundary.
147	- The identity is the **citizen** — rank-neutral, keyfile-backed (federation peer *federate*; payor
148	  founder outside both). Identity lifecycle decoupled from capability lifecycle. Multi-role
149	  first-class.
150	- The citizen tier is one SA (one key, union of capabilities) per person, not one per role —
151	  blast-radius tradeoff accepted.
152	- Capability-sets are named code; grant/revoke operate only on named sets, never raw bindings.
153	- The declared ledger (intent) is part of the model; IAM is enforcement; the audit diffs them.
154	- Audit healing is asymmetric: auto-converge deficits, report surplus; the audit never auto-revokes.
155	- Ledger-write authority is never a capability-set member; the ledger writer for an entry is the
156	  grant-authority holder under the active topology.
157	- Governor breadth is resolved per tier, not globally: keyfile pulls grant authority to the payor /
158	  minimizes the standing governor; federation may keep a condition-limited governor.
159	- The mode enum (keyfile vs federation) belongs in RBRD and is added only when federation lands —
160	  not in this heat.
161	- Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: recover each citizen's role from the role-prefixed
162	  SA name (a one-time bootstrap, distinct from the retired standing "derive from IAM"), register
163	  citizens in the declared ledger, let legacy names age out. No flag day.
164	- The convergence covers operator citizens; cloud-native identities (Mason, future Envoy) share the
165	  capability-set abstraction but not the citizen lifecycle.
166	
167	## Retired cinches
168	
169	- "Roster derives from IAM (an `rbk-identity` label), not a stored list — it cannot drift." Retired:
170	  under decoupling, deriving from IAM is lossy and makes the intended-vs-actual audit a tautology,
171	  and SA labels do not exist (SAs support tags, in Preview). Replaced by the declared ledger +
172	  asymmetric audit. The true invariant it reached for — no enforcement state outside IAM — is
173	  preserved; the false corollary (no intent state may exist) is dropped.
174	
175	## Open — resolve within the heat
176	
177	- Where the Census (declared ledger) physically lives — direction settled: hosted in the **Manor**
178	  (Payor project), where ledger-write authority sits. Remaining: the exact object form (a separate
179	  access-restricted object vs a GAR artifact), explicitly **not** the build bucket (the director
180	  holds objectCreator there); write-ACL held only by governor/payor (the ledger-write invariant;
181	  see The audit).
182	- Whether the keyfile tier drops the standing governor entirely or demotes it to non-privileged —
183	  this choice gates whether project-scope grant-limiting is in-heat.
184	- The citizen orphan marker: SA tags (Preview) vs description sentinel (both advisory; the
185	  authoritative orphan signal is a key on an SA absent from the ledger — see memo).
186	- Whether to offer a combined add+grant convenience wrapper for the common one-hat citizen (the
187	  two-gesture flow itself follows from the decoupling).
188	
189	## Sources
190	
191	rbk-08-credential-repairs; memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races (the lifecycle
192	split — the enabler); memo-20260527-operator-credential-models (the two-tier plan and cult-verb
193	shape — breadcrumbed, full update when frozen); memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak
194	(standalone governor-leak fix); memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model (the mechanism this paddock
195	points to); memo-20260605-citizen-model-ultracode-process (how this revision was produced);
196	RBSHR "Operator federation". This revision folds in the 260605 design conversation and two review
197	passes (single-reviewer + ultracode multi-agent). The 260606 revision adds the Manor↔Census naming,
198	the first-class RBS0 Manor quoin (`rbtgi_manor`), and the verb dispositions above.

## Inherited concern — governor teardown leak

Governor teardown sits inside this heat's blast radius: today's mantle leaks a project tombstone
per re-mantle. The convergence closes it (cheap revoke-before-delete; an idempotent governor is a
further, not-free refinement). Mechanism + standalone fallback if descoped:
memo + memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.

## What done looks like

Every cult-verb surface migrated to the citizen/capability vocabulary: workstation bash (role-keyed
selection collapses to one identity accessor), the governor/payor admin verbs (the admin verbs
collapse to the dissolved set), cloud-side bash naming roles, the .adoc specs voicing the cult
verbs, the Rust theurge cases, the onboarding handbook. "citizen" replaces the role-named identity
throughout, and the SA name itself becomes identity-only (the naming migration: memo).

The load-bearing federation-enabling core is narrow: route the role/file-path-keyed token-accessor
calls through one identity-keyed accessor (generalizing memo-20260527's "single code seam"). The
vocabulary convergence is the larger, motivated-but-not-required remainder. Scoping/heat-chopping is
deferred — recorded here as shape.

## Cinched decisions

- Enforcement is server-side IAM. No file or client-side check is ever the boundary.
- The identity is the **citizen** — rank-neutral, keyfile-backed (federation peer *federate*; payor
  founder outside both). Identity lifecycle decoupled from capability lifecycle. Multi-role
  first-class.
- The citizen tier is one SA (one key, union of capabilities) per person, not one per role —
  blast-radius tradeoff accepted.
- Capability-sets are named code; grant/revoke operate only on named sets, never raw bindings.
- The declared ledger (intent) is part of the model; IAM is enforcement; the audit diffs them.
- Audit healing is asymmetric: auto-converge deficits, report surplus; the audit never auto-revokes.
- Ledger-write authority is never a capability-set member; the ledger writer for an entry is the
  grant-authority holder under the active topology.
- Governor breadth is resolved per tier, not globally: keyfile pulls grant authority to the payor /
  minimizes the standing governor; federation may keep a condition-limited governor.
- The mode enum (keyfile vs federation) belongs in RBRD and is added only when federation lands —
  not in this heat.
- Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: recover each citizen's role from the role-prefixed
  SA name (a one-time bootstrap, distinct from the retired standing "derive from IAM"), register
  citizens in the declared ledger, let legacy names age out. No flag day.
- The convergence covers operator citizens; cloud-native identities (Mason, future Envoy) share the
  capability-set abstraction but not the citizen lifecycle.

## Retired cinches

- "Roster derives from IAM (an `rbk-identity` label), not a stored list — it cannot drift." Retired:
  under decoupling, deriving from IAM is lossy and makes the intended-vs-actual audit a tautology,
  and SA labels do not exist (SAs support tags, in Preview). Replaced by the declared ledger +
  asymmetric audit. The true invariant it reached for — no enforcement state outside IAM — is
  preserved; the false corollary (no intent state may exist) is dropped.

## Open — resolve within the heat

- Where the Census (declared ledger) physically lives — direction settled: hosted in the **Manor**
  (Payor project), where ledger-write authority sits. Remaining: the exact object form (a separate
  access-restricted object vs a GAR artifact), explicitly **not** the build bucket (the director
  holds objectCreator there); write-ACL held only by governor/payor (the ledger-write invariant;
  see The audit).
- Whether the keyfile tier drops the standing governor entirely or demotes it to non-privileged —
  this choice gates whether project-scope grant-limiting is in-heat.
- The citizen orphan marker: SA tags (Preview) vs description sentinel (both advisory; the
  authoritative orphan signal is a key on an SA absent from the ledger — see memo).
- Whether to offer a combined add+grant convenience wrapper for the common one-hat citizen (the
  two-gesture flow itself follows from the decoupling).

## Sources

rbk-08-credential-repairs; memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races (the lifecycle
split — the enabler); memo-20260527-operator-credential-models (the two-tier plan and cult-verb
shape — breadcrumbed, full update when frozen); memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak
(standalone governor-leak fix); memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model (the mechanism this paddock
points to); memo-20260605-citizen-model-ultracode-process (how this revision was produced);
RBSHR "Operator federation". This revision folds in the 260605 design conversation and two review
passes (single-reviewer + ultracode multi-agent). The 260606 revision adds the Manor↔Census naming,
the first-class RBS0 Manor quoin (`rbtgi_manor`), and the verb dispositions above.

content.text:
Findings so far:
- **terrier, muniment, liber, tabularium, patent, rota, fasti, burgess, burgage, codex, cadastre, panoply** → zero concept hits. All open.
- **manifest** → hard conflict (OCI/registry manifest, heavily used). Drop it.
- **ledger** → taken — RBFL *Foundry Ledger* (the reliquary ledger); also the generic word for what Census *is*, so circular. Drop.
- **registry** → taken — GAR / RBGA ArtifactRegistry. Drop.

content.text:
Searched and oriented. Here's where it lands.

**The real driver:** "Census" is semantically *excellent* — the Roman census recorded citizens *and* their rank/class, which is exactly "citizen-roll-with-rank." The problem is purely the letter: **C** collides with Crucible (and the cult C-space generally). So the target is a noun that keeps "a roll of citizens with their standing/rights" but sheds the C.

**My recommendation: `Terrier`** (T).
A terrier is the manorial register of who holds what under the seat — each tenant and the rights/services attached. It pairs cleanly with the freshly-minted **Manor**: *the Manor hosts its Terrier*. That one-implies-the-other resonance is the strongest fit in the set, and it carries both halves of the meaning (who the citizens are **and** what each holds). Zero codebase conflict.

**Close runner-up: `Muniments`** (M) — records preserved as proof of *rights and privileges*. This is the bullseye on the "access protections" half specifically (a Manor's muniment room). Only caution: Manor + Muniments are both M-civic nouns in the same heat — could read as resonance or as muddle.

Alternatives, one line each (all conflict-clear):
- **`Roll`** (R) — the citizen-roll; court/muster roll. Plain, durable, "on the Roll" reads naturally; pairs with enfranchise/expel. Less Manor-resonant than Terrier.
- **`Patent`** (P) — letters patent = granted privilege. Captures the *capability* half hard, the *identity* half weakly — actually a better candidate for naming the **capability-set** than the ledger.
- **`Liber`** (L) — register-book (Liber Albus precedent). Demoted: collides conceptually with **Ledger** (RBFL), both L, both "book."

**Killed by conflict** (so you don't reconsider them): `Manifest` (OCI manifest), `Registry` (GAR), `Ledger` (RBFL Foundry Ledger), `Writ` (rbw-cw). The d/c/a-initial civic words are out by your rule (Census, Codex, Cadastre, Domesday, Album).

aiTitle: Rename federation ledger from census to better term

content.text:
Pulled both. Here's what they actually are today, and the verdict.

**`Writ`** — *out, doubly.* It's the entrenched **sentry-exec operation**: `rbob_writ` / `rbw-cw`, "Writ to sentry," the sibling of `bark` (exec in bottle) and `fiat` (exec in pentacle). It's used ~20 times across the theurge crucible tests (`rbtdrc_crucible.rs`). Beyond the collision, the *meaning* is wrong: a writ is an **issued order/command**, not a register of members — the legal sense ("a formal command from authority") maps to "dispatch a command into a container," exactly as the code uses it. It's a verb-flavored action concept, not a roll. Not better than Terrier; drop with confidence.

**`Ledger`** — *out, but for a more interesting reason worth seeing.* `Ledger` is **RBFL "Foundry Ledger"**: a full module family (rbfl*, its own CLI) that is the **registry of reliquaries** — the record of which tool-image cohorts exist. Note it's not a formal RBS0 cult quoin; it's the module name plus the paddock's own generic descriptor ("the declared ledger," "ledger-write authority"). Two distinct reasons it's occupied:

1. RBFL already owns it as a module name.
2. The paddock uses "ledger" as the common noun for *what Census is* — so promoting it to Census's proper name shadows the generic word.

But here's the wrinkle that actually **strengthens Terrier**: your civic ontology splits **operators (citizens)** from **holdings (arks/hoards/bullions)**. The Foundry Ledger is already the *ledger of holdings*. So "ledger" is the **genus**, Foundry Ledger is the *holdings* species, and the citizen registry wants its own *distinct* proper name sitting **beside** Ledger, not on top of it. That's exactly the Census/Terrier slot. Reusing "Ledger" would erase a clean genus/species symmetry you already have.

**Net:** neither is better — both are occupied *and* Writ is semantically wrong. Terrier still leads, and the Ledger analysis is a point in its favor, not against. (One honest caveat on Terrier: historically a terrier is a register of a manor's *landholdings* — so it leans holdings-ish too. It still works read as "tenant + what-they-hold → citizen + capabilities-they-hold," but if you want the people-side emphasis unmistakable, **Muniments** or **Roll** carry it more cleanly.)

Want me to lock Terrier into the paddock, or pull the Muniments/Roll comparison up one more notch first?

## 5. Vocabulary

The roadmap's working words `chantry` (target) and `conduit` (reach) both start with `C`,
which stumbles against Crucible / Census. Renamed to unbound primary letters:

| Concept | Noun | Verbs | Register | Cardinality |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| The model endpoint a bottle consumes | **Quire** | `endow` / `dissolve` | ecclesiastical (heir to *chantry*'s "where the voice issues") | 0..N |
| The peer-bearing private reach into an AWS VPC | **Outpost** | `raise` / `strike` | camp/diplomatic (sibling to *embassy*) | 0..1 |

All four verbs are collision-free across RB and JJK today (`grep` lands clean — the
findability rationale the operator asked us to favor).

**Two tiers of lifecycle.** The Outpost is the heavy, shared, singleton reach (raise once,
strike once). Quires are the light bindings that ride it (endow/dissolve freely). This is the
conduit-vs-target split, resolved: the reach object is first-class, not footprint — its 0..1
cardinality is exactly what earns it a noun and its own verbs.

**The peer is *not* separately named.** It is the Outpost's compute; `raise`/`strike` operate
on the Outpost, which includes the peer. No peer-lifecycle verbs — the peer is hourly-cheap,
stop/start saves only noise, and the endpoint/EIP are standing anyway.

**Prefix allocation (open minting item).** A Quire regime is a natural sibling to
nameplate/vessel; `RBRQ` is a free slot in the `RBR*` family. The Outpost cannot be `RBRO`
(taken by the OAuth regime), so its config home needs a non-`RBRO` slot — deferred to the
later minting pass (which also owns the unrelated Census/Crucible `C` collision).

content.text:
That `pwd`/interrupt didn't lose anything — and the grep just changed my answer. I owe you a correction first: I called memo-20260605 "clean / no action," but that was only true for the word *Census*. You're right that it's federation material — and it's the **heaviest "ledger" user of all: 40 hits**, vs. 2 in federation-canon and 1 in bedrock-quire. I undersold it.

Here's the crux the numbers expose:

**"Census" and "ledger" aren't two synonyms competing for one slot — they're two *registers* of the same thing.**
- **"declared ledger"** is the **mechanism-layer term of art**, defined in memo-20260605 ("*Declared ledger* — depot-resident data mapping identity → {capability-sets held}… short form: *the ledger*"). It carries the audit invariant, the verb table (`read-ledger`, `ledger-write authority`, "withdraw the ledger"), the audit axes ("non-ledger principal"). It's load-bearing.
- **"Census"** is the **cult/civic name**, layered on top later (the 260606 pass). It's the spec-surface word.

This project deliberately runs *cult-name over mechanism-word*: **Manor** over "the Payor project," **Reliquary** over "datestamped GAR namespace." So "Terrier" over "declared ledger" is the *same pattern* — which reframes your question from "keep or kill" into "**which word is the name, and which is just English describing what kind of thing it is.**"

**Census → zero.** Confident, no fork. It's the retired cult name; every live mention migrates to Terrier (plus the bedrock memo's "C-collision" item, now *resolved*). Git history is the breadcrumb; at most one retired-cinch line in the paddock.

**"ledger" → a real fork, and I won't pretend otherwise:**

- **Option 1 (my pick): Terrier is the sole *name*; "ledger" survives only as a lowercase common noun describing its data-nature.** Define it once ("the **Terrier** — a depot-resident declared ledger of citizen → capability-sets"), then "Terrier" is the name everywhere it's *referred to*, and "ledger" stays only where it's honestly describing the thing's shape (data-vs-code, intent-first). The contamination you're worried about — bare "the ledger" colliding with Foundry Ledger — only bit where "ledger" was standing in as a *name*; Terrier now occupies that slot, so the collision's already cured. Lowest-violence, consistent with Manor/Reliquary.

- **Option 2 (purist): purge both words.** Terrier stands wholly alone — a terrier *is* a register of holdings, so it needs no "ledger" gloss any more than Reliquary needs "registry." Every compound converts: `read-ledger`→read the Terrier, `ledger-write authority`→Terrier-write authority, "non-ledger principal"→"a principal absent from the Terrier." Cleanest one-word outcome; costs a full rewrite of memo-20260605's 40 sites and a couple of clunky compounds ("Terrier-write authority" reads worse than "ledger-write authority").

The swing cases between the two are exactly the **term-of-art compounds** (`read-ledger`, `ledger-write`, "ledger withdrawal"). Everything else lands the same.

I'd take Option 1: Terrier as the name, Census gone, "ledger" demoted from candidate-name to plain descriptor. Which way do you want it?

aiTitle: Rename federation ledger from census to better term

content.text:
Both questions resolved, and the answer is clean: **it's a cosmetic, docs-only rename — not load-bearing.**

## "ledger" in code: zero federation footprint

Four independent checks all agree the *federation* ledger (Census / declared-ledger) has **no executable presence**:
- **Quoin tokens** in specs: none (`:x_ledger:` / `[[x_ledger]]` → empty).
- **Code identifiers**: one hit, and it's not ours.
- **git -S history**: the strings "ledger" *and* "census" have **never appeared in any rbk spec, ever** (`RBS0` included).
- **Config/env/json**: none.

Every "ledger" that *does* live in code belongs to a **different kit's ledger** — leave all three alone:

| File / id | Owner | Not federation because |
|---|---|---|
| `Tools/vok/vol_ledger.json`, `ZVOB_LEDGER_FILE` | VOK build | `vol_` = VOK Ledger, build provenance |
| `Tools/jjk/vov_veiled/jjl_ledger.json` | JJK | `jjl_` = JJK Ledger |
| `rbfl0_FoundryLedger.sh` + rbfl family | RBFL | reliquary registry (the holdings ledger) |

So Census→Terrier and the ledger-word decision touch **only memos + the ₣BZ paddock**. No function, variable, filename, quoin, or config changes. The "Foundry Ledger contamination" we discussed is purely a *prose* concern, never a code collision.

## Your quoin memory — found it, but it's a different ledger

Commit **`₢A4AAP`** (`f02befb56`, 2026-04-05): *"Remove arcanum and ledger — all functionality superseded by MCP tool."* That eliminated the **JJK** ledger concept; the filename `jjl_ledger.json` survived (now under `vov_veiled/`). That's exactly the "quoin eliminated, filename reference remained" pattern you're recalling — but it was **JJK's** ledger, not the federation Census. The federation ledger never had a quoin or a file to leave behind.

## One adjacent *real* edit (not the rename, but in Terrier's path)

`RBSDR-director_roster.adoc` is still live (Jun 1) and **doesn't yet mark itself retired**, even though memo-20260605 and the paddock retire the roster concept ("dissolves into reading the declared ledger"). When the Terrier work lands, that roster → *read-the-Terrier* dissolution is a genuine spec edit — but it's separable from the rename and not part of "emplace Terrier for Census."

**Net:** the rename is low-risk. So the only open decision is still the prose one — Option 1 (Terrier = name, "ledger" demoted to descriptor) vs Option 2 (purge "ledger" too). Want to settle that and have me proceed?

message.content: I think option 2, as long as you aren't referring to one of the other kit ledgers accidentally.

content.text:

Let me read memo-20260605 in full — it's the definitional home (40 hits) and where the compound reworks (`read-ledger`, `ledger-write authority`, etc.) need care:

content.content:
1	# Citizen Tier — Identity / Capability Mechanics
2	
3	Date: 2026-06-05
4	
5	Status: Design captured during the 260605 grooming conversation for heat ₣BZ
6	(`rbk-14-citizen-model`). The *shape* lives in the ₣BZ paddock; this memo holds the
7	*mechanism* the paddock points to. Nothing here is built yet. Amended 260605 after two review
8	passes (a single-reviewer pass, then an ultracode multi-agent pass — see
9	memo-20260605-citizen-model-ultracode-process.md).
10	
11	## Scope and vocabulary
12	
13	This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current, keyfile, no-org operator-credential
14	tier — restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's, so
15	that federation plugs in later by swapping identity-kind and token mint and nothing else.
16	
17	- **Citizen** — a keyfile-tier operator identity: a depot-issued service account holding one
18	  RBRA key. Rank-neutral (governor, director, retriever are all citizens); the home of the
19	  identity, not the role. Possession of the key is the identity. (Mints over RBSHR's prose use of
20	  "citizen" for a depot *artifact* — those are reworded to "holding"; minting rule: semantic
21	  uniqueness across all persistent names.)
22	- **Federate** (future, not this heat) — a federation-tier operator identity: an external-IdP
23	  `principal://` subject the depot recognizes by treaty. A federate holds no key. (This is the
24	  *Workforce*-federated operator subject; not the envoy's *Workload* "federates-to-AWS" egress,
25	  which RBSHR Envoy and memo-20260527 Workforce-vs-Workload already separate.)
26	- **Payor** — the founding human (OAuth refresh token), outside both tiers; neither citizen nor
27	  federate (memo-20260527).
28	- **Capability-set** — a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (the role's binding list,
29	  lifted out of today's invest bodies). Tier-blind.
30	- **Declared ledger** — depot-resident data mapping identity → {capability-sets held}: the stored
31	  *intent*. Tier-blind. Distinct from today's `roster` verb (RBSDR), which name-regex-reads the
32	  *actual* IAM state — that verb is retired here (no role prefix to filter on). The **audit**
33	  performs the actual-state read; the audit is precisely the diff between **actual IAM state** and
34	  the declared ledger, and routine "who holds what" is the ledger read. (Short form below: "the
35	  ledger.")
36	- **Holdings** — the depot's artifacts (arks/hoards/bullions). Property, not identities. The
37	  depot's civic ontology — citizens/federates (people) + holdings (property) — covers *operator*
38	  identities; cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it, reusing the
39	  capability-set abstraction without the citizen lifecycle (whether they deserve their own
40	  ontological category is deferred).
41	
42	The convergence: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke, the declared ledger,
43	the audit) is tier-blind and shared. The **identity** differs by tier (citizen vs federate) —
44	that difference is the one real seam. "Structural isomorphism" means the capability layer is
45	identical across tiers *modulo the opaque principal handle the identity layer supplies*
46	(`serviceAccount:…` vs `principal://…`); only identity-kind, that handle, and the token mint
47	(signed-JWT vs STS) differ.
48	
49	## Verb dissolution
50	
51	Today's role-keyed verbs decompose into a generic register parameterized by
52	(actor, capability-set, identity):
53	
54	| Today | Decomposes to | Notes |
55	|---|---|---|
56	| invest (director/retriever) | add-citizen + grant(set) | governor is actor |
57	| mantle (governor) | add-citizen + grant(governor-set) | payor is actor; not special *once the governor is idempotent* (see below) |
58	| divest | revoke(all held) + remove-citizen | revoke is intent-first (withdraw ledger, then unbind); remove-citizen then deletes the SA |
59	| roster | read-ledger (routine) + actual-state read folds into the audit | the old name-regex roster is retired — no prefix to filter |
60	| — | grant(set) / revoke(set) | the capability axis, tier-blind; each intent-first |
61	| — | read-ledger | read the declared ledger (routine "who holds what", tier-blind) |
62	| — | rekey | keyfile-only identity-backing maintenance |
63	
64	Actor and capability-set are parameters, not verb variants. Federation reuses the capability
65	half (grant/revoke/ledger-read) verbatim and swaps the identity half (add-federate, no rekey).
66	
67	**mantle is not special only once the governor is idempotent** (stable name, add-once + rekey). If
68	the governor stays datestamped delete+recreate (today's RBSGM), mantle decomposes to
69	*divest + add-citizen + grant* and remains a replace verb — see Governor teardown leak.
70	
71	## Identity verbs branch; capability verbs do not
72	
73	- **Capability verbs (grant/revoke)** never branch on tier — both are an IAM binding on a
74	  pre-existing identity. Both are **intent-first**: grant writes the ledger entry then adds
75	  bindings; revoke withdraws the ledger entry then removes them. Invariant: *no IAM revoke without a
76	  prior ledger withdrawal* — a crashed teardown then lands as a transient *surplus* (reported,
77	  safe), not an auto-converging *deficit* that would resurrect the just-removed identity (acute
78	  because RBSDD anticipates immediate same-folio re-invest).
79	- **Identity verbs** are tier-specific (this is the seam):
80	  - *citizen* (this heat):
81	    - **add** = mint SA + key + deliver RBRA.
82	    - **rekey** = rotate the key, bindings untouched. Availability-safe order: create-new →
83	      deliver RBRA → verify → delete-old (never delete-before-create — a failed create would leave
84	      a live citizen keyless). GCP caps an SA at **10 keys** (hard, non-adjustable), so a rekey
85	      that fails to reclaim the old key accumulates toward the ceiling; reclaim stale keys, and let
86	      the orphan/audit sweep flag `>1` USER_MANAGED key on a citizen as a stranded key to reclaim.
87	    - **remove** = delete the SA (and key). Capabilities are revoked first by divest's
88	      revoke(all held), which is intent-first (see Capability verbs), so the full teardown order is
89	      withdraw-ledger → unbind-IAM → delete-SA.
90	  - *federate* (future): add ≈ first grant (identity owned by the IdP, not minted here); no rekey
91	    (the IdP owns rotation); remove = de-register + revoke-all (the principal is not ours to
92	    delete).
93	  - *payor*: install / refresh a human OAuth refresh token.
94	- Token mint also branches (citizen: signed-JWT; federate: STS) and sits below everything.
95	
96	## The declared ledger
97	
98	Why intent must be stored (the retired "derive from IAM" cinch was self-defeating once the role
99	prefix leaves the identity name):
100	
101	1. Without a name prefix, deriving the ledger from IAM is lossy reverse-mapping — a director set
102	   is several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket; partial and ambiguous
103	   matches become representable.
104	2. You cannot audit IAM against itself — "intent = whatever IAM says" makes intended-vs-actual a
105	   tautology that always passes, discarding the drift signal.
106	
107	Federation forces the same ledger anyway (a federated principal carries no role marker;
108	memo-20260527: "the depot remains the home of the roster"), so the ledger is paid once for both
109	tiers and makes the routine ledger read identical and O(1) across tiers.
110	
111	- **Code vs data.** Capability-set *definitions* are code; the ledger is *data* (memberships).
112	  The audit compares: for each (identity, declared-set), are the code-defined bindings present in
113	  IAM?
114	- **Physical home (open) — a security boundary, not just storage.** The home must place
115	  ledger-write authority at least on par with grant authority (see the audit invariant below).
116	  This **disqualifies the obvious choice**: a GCS object in the depot's *build* bucket fails the
117	  invariant, because the director already holds `roles/storage.objectCreator` there (RBSDK) — a
118	  director could overwrite the ledger and self-escalate via auto-converge. The ledger home's
119	  write-ACL must be reachable by **no capability-set** — held only by governor/payor. Viable
120	  options: a separate access-restricted bucket/object, or a GAR artifact, each with a
121	  governor/payor-only write ACL. Decide in-heat. **Cinch: ledger-write authority is never a
122	  capability-set member.**
123	- **Writers (topology-conditional).** The ledger writer for an entry is the holder of grant
124	  authority for that entry's capability-sets under the active topology.
125	  - Federation/multi-admin: the governor writes director/retriever entries; the payor writes the
126	    governor entry.
127	  - Keyfile/solo: the payor writes director/retriever entries directly; a demoted or absent
128	    governor must NOT write entries it lacks grant authority for.
129	
130	## The audit
131	
132	Drift taxonomy (for one identity; D = union of declared sets' bindings, A = actual RBK-relevant
133	bindings):
134	
135	| State | Meaning | Likely cause | Heal |
136	|---|---|---|---|
137	| D = A | clean | — | — |
138	| deficit-whole | a declared set wholly absent in A | grant never ran / errant revoke | auto-converge |
139	| deficit-fragment | a declared set partly present | half-failed multi-scope grant | auto-converge |
140	| surplus-complete | a whole undeclared named set in A | out-of-band grant | report |
141	| surplus-fragment | bindings belonging to no whole set | tampering / shrunk definition / half-failed revoke | report (but see below) |
142	
143	**Half-failed revoke is ours, not adversarial.** A multi-scope revoke (a director set spans
144	project + Mason + repo + bucket) that fails partway — exactly the Class-C 403-flap memo-20260604
145	documents — leaves a fragment of a no-longer-declared set: structurally a surplus, but *ours*. The
146	divest/remove verb retries its own revoke idempotently (Class-C tolerance) so most flaps resolve
147	(~81s) without stranding a fragment; for a fully abandoned mid-divest, a recorded "revoking" intent
148	marker lets the audit recognize an interrupted authorized revoke as ours and finish it
149	idempotently, distinct from out-of-band surplus.
150	
151	**Asymmetric healing = the Pale recognition test:**
152	
153	- **Deficit is ours** (our own grant failed to complete) → auto-converge up to D (re-run the
154	  idempotent grant), logged, never silent.
155	- **Surplus crossed the Pale** (IAM touched outside our verbs) → characterize, report, let a human
156	  adjudicate (legitimize → declare it, or reject → revoke it). Never auto-backfill intent
157	  (re-enters the tautology). **The *audit* never auto-revokes** — surplus resolution is
158	  human-driven. (Divest/remove *do* revoke; that is an explicit operator teardown, not an audit
159	  heal.)
160	
161	**Invariant the asymmetry rests on.** Auto-converging deficits treats the declared ledger as
162	authoritative intent, so writing the ledger *upward* is itself a grant. The auto-heal is safe only
163	if ledger-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (setIamPolicy); otherwise it
164	is a softer escalation path than setIamPolicy. The ledger's physical home (above) must satisfy that
165	ACL constraint — a security boundary, not a storage convenience.
166	
167	**Two audit axes.** The identity-first axis (above) walks `ledger identities × set-defined
168	bindings`. It **cannot see** escalation onto a *fresh, non-ledger* principal — e.g. a leaked
169	director, via repo-scope `repoAdmin`/`setIamPolicy`, grants a role to a brand-new SA the ledger
170	never names. So the audit also runs a **member-first axis**: on every resource where an RBK
171	principal can reach `setIamPolicy` (the AR repo, the build bucket, individual SAs), enumerate the
172	*actual* members and flag any RBK-relevant binding whose member is neither a ledger identity nor an
173	allow-listed system identity (Mason, payor, governor, future Envoy — allow-listed to avoid false
174	positives). The repoAdmin residual (Authority section) is covered **only once this axis exists**.
175	
176	Consequences:
177	
178	- Because grant writes intent *first*, our tooling cannot produce a surplus *absent a definition
179	  change* — so a surplus is normally a clean "someone bypassed the verbs" signal. The one
180	  tooling-side surplus source is a capability-set definition *shrink* (next bullet), which strands a
181	  binding our own earlier grant placed — which is why surplus is reported, not auto-revoked,
182	  regardless of origin.
183	- **A definition change is not all routine.** A *shrink* decomposes to surplus-on-removals (report
184	  for human-confirmed revoke). An *expansion* (adding a binding to a set's definition) turns every
185	  holder into a deficit the audit would auto-grant — silently relocating the privilege-expansion
186	  trust boundary to "who can merge a one-line code change." So distinguish a per-identity deficit
187	  against an *unchanged* definition (a genuine failed grant — safe to auto-heal) from a deficit
188	  appearing across *all* holders of a set at once (the signature of an expansion — gate behind the
189	  same human adjudication that surplus-legitimization requires). **"Who may edit capability-set
190	  definitions" is a first-class authority, equal to grant / ledger-write.**
191	- **Concurrency.** Two writers hit IAM: the grant verb and the audit's deficit auto-converge.
192	  Auto-converge re-reads the ledger under etag immediately before each grant (not from a
193	  start-of-audit snapshot), so a concurrent revoke wins. Both teardown sides (revoke, definition
194	  shrink) write the ledger first so a crash lands as report-only surplus, never an auto-converging
195	  deficit that resurrects what was just removed.
196	- **Audit domain.** Identity-first: ledger identities × set-defined bindings. Member-first: actual
197	  members of setIamPolicy-reachable resource policies. A non-ledger, non-system member is *flagged*,
198	  not ignored.
199	
200	## Orphan hazard and the identity marker (citizen tier only)
201	
202	With no name prefix and no SA labels, nothing intrinsic marks an SA as RBK-managed — so a failed
203	add-citizen can strand a live key for an untracked SA (a credential leak you cannot find).
204	
205	The orphan sweep is **integrity-advisory, not authoritative.** The description sentinel lives in
206	`serviceAccounts.update` — the same `serviceAccountAdmin` authority that mints citizens — so it can
207	be stripped, or absent after a crash. Marker *absence must not be trusted.* The **authoritative**
208	orphan signal is the ledger cross-check the capability audit already performs: a USER_MANAGED key on
209	an SA absent from the declared ledger is an orphan, marker or not. Marker options, used only as
210	corroborating hints:
211	
212	- **SA tags** (tag bindings): key-value, listable, condition-usable, with a separable (stronger)
213	  tag-binding ACL — preferred once they leave **Preview**.
214	- **SA `description` sentinel** (`rbk:…`): stable fallback for the sweep; the ledger owns
215	  capability, the description earns exactly this one hint.
216	
217	Federation has no key to leak and a stray principal binding is visible in the policy, so this
218	hazard is citizen-tier-specific.
219	
220	## Authority — who may grant which capability-set
221	
222	- **Resource-scope-split principle.** An authority boundary is IAM-real only where it coincides
223	  with a resource boundary. Payor/governor holds because billing is a separate resource that
224	  governor's `roles/owner` excludes. Governor/director does not — both live in project IAM and
225	  governor has owner over it — so "governor may grant only director/retriever" is, by itself,
226	  honor-system.
227	- **IAM can scope grants by role** (corrects the first paddock draft). The condition attribute
228	  `iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole` limits which roles a setIamPolicy-capable principal
229	  may grant/revoke:
230	  `api.getAttribute('iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole', []).hasOnly(['roles/x','roles/y'])`.
231	  Limits: works only on **project/folder/org** allow policies, **not** resource-level (AR repo,
232	  bucket, individual SA); ≤10 roles per condition; don't join `hasOnly` with `&&`/`||`; some
233	  services don't recognize the limit (request fails); the governor must NOT hold plain
234	  `roles/owner` (unconditional setIamPolicy) — give it a conditioned setIamPolicy instead.
235	  Google's own caveat — don't let a limited admin grant roles bearing setIamPolicy — is satisfied
236	  at *project* scope: the project-scoped director/retriever grants bear no setIamPolicy. The
237	  repo-scoped `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` *does* carry
238	  `artifactregistry.repositories.setIamPolicy` — that is the resource-scope residual covered by the
239	  audit's **member-first axis** (the identity-first axis alone cannot see a grant to a fresh
240	  principal), and modifiedGrantsByRole does not reach repo scope anyway.
241	- **Composed resolution.**
242	  - Project-scoped grants → IAM-limit on the governor (preventive, server-side).
243	  - Resource-scoped grants (repo/bucket/SA) → not IAM-limitable → detective audit (member-first
244	    axis) covers the residual.
245	  - Topology → pull grant authority to the payor where a standing governor buys nothing.
246	- **Tier-dependent.** Keyfile/solo (payor == admin) → topology: a standing owner-holding citizen
247	  buys nothing and is pure attack surface; drop or demote it. Federation/multi-admin (no
248	  exfiltrable key) → a condition-limited governor is the right preventive tool.
249	- **Audit ↔ topology seam.** Surplus-detection's security value depends on topology: a broad
250	  standing governor makes surplus-detection the only escalation tripwire (detective-only, weak vs
251	  malice); authority at the payor closes escalation structurally and demotes the audit to
252	  honest-mistake hygiene. Do not substitute detective audit for structural closure.
253	- **Ephemeral governor** (decoupling-enabled option): grant the governor-set for one op, then
254	  revoke. Caveat: because capability-revoke is decoupled from key-destruction, this strips the
255	  *binding* but leaves the on-disk key — to shrink the keyfile attack surface you must couple
256	  key-destruction (rekey-to-nothing or SA delete) to the revoke; and during grant→use→revoke plus
257	  revoke lag (~81s, memo-20260604) the on-disk key carries full owner. Relevant only if a keyfile
258	  delegated governor is ever wanted.
259	
260	## Blast radius — full reasoning
261	
262	One citizen per person = one SA = one key carrying the **union** of granted capabilities. This is
263	**forced** by "one identity per person": an SA's key always wields the SA's full bindings; you
264	cannot scope one key to a subset. The only escape — separate SAs per role — is the old model,
265	which discards the citizen goal. So accepting multi-role on one citizen *is* accepting the union
266	key; they are one decision.
267	
268	- Cost: larger resting target (a leaked key carries more).
269	- Bounded: the *resting-key* form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only (a file to steal);
270	  federation has no key at rest. The union-of-capabilities-on-one-identity property is itself
271	  tier-blind — a federate granted director+retriever wields both too, exposed through a hijacked
272	  live session rather than a file at rest. Most citizens wear one hat (one-per-person ==
273	  one-per-role).
274	- Upside: finer incident response — revoke a capability without touching the citizen/key, or rekey
275	  without touching capabilities. The old model's only lever was delete-and-recreate.
276	
277	## Governor teardown leak (folds in)
278	
279	`rbgp_governor_mantle` deletes outgoing `governor-*` SAs without first revoking their grants,
280	minting a `deleted:…?uid=` tombstone per re-mantle. The leak closes cheaply: add the generic
281	revoke-before-delete (reusing the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C propagation tolerance) before the
282	existing delete — no tombstone, no naming change. The further idempotent-governor refinement (rekey
283	instead of delete+recreate, dropping the delete entirely) is *not* free: it needs a *stable*
284	governor name in place of today's datestamped `governor-YYYYMMDDHHmm` (which exists precisely to
285	dodge the delete→recreate flap), plus a one-time migration of existing datestamped governors.
286	Standalone leak fix (if the heat descopes the governor):
287	memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.
288	
289	## Citizen naming migration
290	
291	The model's foundation is the SA name becoming **identity-only** (`alice`, not `director-alice`) —
292	the precondition for one-citizen-one-key-union and for the lossy-IAM-derivation argument. (The
293	governor's analogous rename is in Governor teardown leak; this is the director/retriever dual.)
294	Surfaces:
295	
296	- **RBSDK / RBSRK** — SA-name composition drops the role prefix (was `${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>`).
297	- **RBSDD** — email synthesis becomes identity-only.
298	- **RBSDR** — the prefix-filter roster is retired in favor of the declared-ledger read.
299	- **RBSRA / RBSRR** — the three role-keyed `rbra.env` paths collapse toward one identity-keyed path.
300	
301	**Existing-SA cutover.** GCP cannot rename an SA, so legacy `director-*`/`retriever-*` SAs migrate by
302	delete-old + invest-new, re-entering the divest revoke-before-delete + the invest create/propagation
303	race (the memo-20260604 churn) — it must run through the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C tolerance.
304	
305	**One-time bootstrap** (distinct from the *retired* standing "derive from IAM"): recover each legacy
306	citizen's role — and thus its capability-set — from the role-prefixed SA **name** (the RBSDR roster
307	regex), reading bindings only to seed/confirm; thereafter the ledger stands independently. Stamp the
308	orphan marker onto each grandfathered SA so the sweep operates on a marker-uniform domain (a
309	back-stamp prerequisite — until then sweep coverage of the legacy population is incomplete).
310	
311	Whether *this heat* performs the migration is under the open scoping flag (the paddock).
312	
313	## Verified facts (checked 260605)
314	
315	- **Service accounts do not support resource labels.** They support **tags** (tag bindings),
316	  currently in Preview. So the orphan marker uses tags (Preview) or the description sentinel, not a
317	  label.
318	- **IAM can limit which roles a principal may grant** via `modifiedGrantsByRole`, but only on
319	  project/folder/org policies and ≤10 roles — not on resource-level policies.
320	- **A service account is capped at 10 keys** (hard, non-adjustable) — bounds the rekey lifecycle.
321	- A service account **can exist with zero IAM bindings** (the decoupling primitive).
322	
323	Sources:
324	- Set limits on granting roles — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles
325	- Tags for service accounts — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags
326	- Service accounts overview — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-overview
327	
328	## memo-20260527 divergence (record when the model is frozen)
329	
330	memo-20260527 states the operator verbs "keep their names, only their bodies differ," and that
331	keyfile holds "one SA key file each per role." This heat supersedes both: the verb *bodies*
332	converge in shape (not just names), and the citizen tier is one SA *per person*, not per role.
333	
334	A third divergence the paddock leans on by name: 20260527's "single code seam" is a *role-keyed*
335	token accessor ("a token as director"), shippable with no behavior change over today's per-role
336	SAs. Under one-SA-per-person the accessor becomes *identity-keyed* ("a token as alice", carrying the
337	union) — a generalization of that seam, but *not* itself no-behavior-change, since it presupposes
338	the per-person migration. (Whether the heat's "load-bearing core" is the role-keyed no-behavior
339	stepping-stone or the identity-keyed end state is an open scoping question — see the paddock.)
340	
341	A deferral-honoring breadcrumb has been added to memo-20260527's Status block now; the full
342	divergence note folds in when the model is frozen.
